title_en: The Dawn of Prophethood — The First Revelation
content_en:
The period immediately preceding the first revelation was marked by a deepening of the Prophet's ﷺ spiritual solitude and an intensifying of his practice of retreat (khalwa) in the Cave of Hira on a mountain outside Mecca. Al-Mubarakpuri describes how, as Muhammad ﷺ approached his fortieth year, he was drawn increasingly to periods of contemplation away from the noise and moral corruption of Meccan society. He would spend days at a time in Hira, bringing provisions for sustained retreat, engaged in a form of worship — though the precise form is debated by scholars — that reflected his innate monotheistic disposition.

Before the first revelation, the Prophet ﷺ experienced a transitional period in which he began to receive 'true visions' (al-ru'ya al-sadiqah) — dreams that proved to be accurate predictors of events, described as being like 'the dawn breaking.' These preliminary experiences are understood by scholars as part of the gradual preparation of the Prophet's ﷺ faculties for the tremendous weight of revelation.

Then came the night that would change human history. In the month of Ramadan, in the year 610 CE, as Muhammad ﷺ was in solitary retreat in the Cave of Hira, the Angel Jibril (Gabriel) appeared to him. Jibril embraced him forcefully and commanded: 'Read!' (Iqra'). The Prophet ﷺ replied: 'I cannot read' (or: 'What shall I read?'). Jibril embraced him again, and again commanded 'Read!' and again the Prophet ﷺ gave the same reply. The third time, Jibril recited: 'Read in the name of your Lord who created — created man from a clinging substance. Read, and your Lord is the most Generous — who taught by the pen, taught man that which he knew not' (Quran 96:1-5). These were the first words of revelation — the first verses of what would become the Quran.

The Prophet ﷺ descended from the mountain, his heart trembling, and rushed home to his wife Khadijah bint Khuwaylid. He said: 'Wrap me up! Wrap me up!' — and she covered him until the fear passed. He then told her what had happened, expressing his fear that something terrible had befallen him. Khadijah responded with words that have become among the most celebrated in Islamic history: 'Never! By Allah, Allah will never disgrace you. You uphold the ties of kinship, you speak truthfully, you carry the burdens of others, you are generous to guests, and you help people in their distress.'

Khadijah then took him to her cousin Waraqah ibn Nawfal, an elderly man who had studied the scriptures of Christianity and Judaism. Waraqah listened to the account and declared: 'This is the Namus (the angel who brings revelation) that Allah sent to Musa. I wish I were young and could live to see the time when your people will drive you out.' The Prophet ﷺ was astonished — would his own people drive him out? 'Yes,' said Waraqah. 'No man has ever come with what you have come with except that he was treated with hostility. If I live to see your time, I will support you strongly.'

Waraqah died shortly afterward, and the revelations paused for a period (al-fatrah), during which the Prophet ﷺ experienced profound distress and longing for the return of the angel. Then Jibril returned, and the revelation resumed — this time with no cessation until the final verse was revealed shortly before the Prophet's ﷺ death, twenty-three years later.
